heading: libro intitolato Dio è allo stesso tempo nascosto e manifesto, afferma:
card_summary: This chunk from the second Canticle of De Harmonia Mundi opens with a passage from Hermes Trismegistus (De intellectu communi) on God as simultaneously hidden and manifest, omnipresent in all bodies. Zorzi aligns this with John 1:3 ("omnia per ipsum facta sunt"), Orphic theology, and Manilius's Astronomica on the World-Soul permeating the cosmos. He then draws on Calcidius's commentary on Plato's Timaeus to explain why the world-animal, though animated, does not decay. Subsequent chapters trace the dual sources of natural and spiritual life (Jeremiah's "fons aquarum viventium," Genesis's river of Eden, Psalms, Ezekiel, and John's Gospel), invoke Apuleius of Madaura as a Platonic witness to divine omnipresence, and develop an extended allegory of Christ as the Tree of Life, drawing on Isaiah, Plato's doctrine of self-motion, and Kabbalistic readings of the Hebrew mi-qedem ("from the beginning").
essay:
## Note on Coverage

This summary covers the opening portion of chunk 70 (pp. 1309–1394, approximately the first 300 lines of the file as read). The full section is 31,817 words; only the opening segment has been read per the stated methodology. Further sub-chapters beyond those visible in this sample are not summarised here.

---

## Central Theme: Divine Omnipresence, the World-Soul, and the Tree of Life

Chunk 70 belongs to the second Canticle (*Cantico Secondo*, *Tono Secondo*) of Zorzi's *De Harmonia Mundi*. Its overarching concern is the pervasiveness of God in creation — simultaneously hidden and manifest — traced through Hermetic, Orphic, Platonic, Scriptural, and Kabbalistic sources. The section then develops this into a theology of life: all natural and spiritual life flows from a single divine fountain, identified as the Trinitarian God, and Christ is expounded as the Tree of Life at the centre of the Archetype.

---

## I. Hermes Trismegistus: Deus Absconditus et Manifestus

Zorzi opens by quoting a Hermetic source — identified in context as a *libro intitolato Dio è allo stesso tempo nascosto e manifesto* (likely the *Asclepius* or a related Hermetic tractate) — on the paradox of divine hiddenness and manifestation:

> «Non vi è nulla nei corpi che non sia Dio; egli solo fa esistere tutte le cose, portando alla luce tutto ciò che è e nascondendo in se stesso ciò che non è. Egli è nascosto, egli è il più manifesto di tutti. Egli è oggetto chiarissimo per la mente ed è visibile agli occhi; è incorporeo e, per così dire, multicorporeo»

<cite>p. 1309 (opening lines of chunk)</cite>

A second Hermetic citation, from *De intellectu communi*, follows: "Non esiste alcunché che non sia Dio stesso" — God is the life of all things. Zorzi presents this as a convergence with the Johannine prologue.

---

## II. John 1:3 and the Double Sense of "Nothing"

Zorzi immediately cross-references this Hermetic declaration with John 1:3 (*omnia per ipsum facta sunt, et sine ipso factum est nihil*), which he reads in a double sense: either nothing was made without the Logos, or whatever was made without his conserving, filling, and vivifying action is as nothing (*nihil est*). This double hermeneutic — a literal and a participative reading — is characteristic of Zorzi's method throughout *De Harmonia Mundi*.

---

## III. Orpheus and Manilius: The World-Soul

Zorzi invokes Orphic theology as teaching that God is present and visible "in tutte le membra del corpo mondano." This is then illustrated at length by a passage from Manilius's *Astronomica* (quoted in the Latin original and given in Italian translation):

> «Cum spiritus unus / Per cunctas habitet partes, atque irriget orbem / Omnia pervolitans, corpusque animale figuret… / Hic igitur Deus, et ratio, quae cuncta gubernat.»

<cite>pp. 1309–1310 (Manilius, Astronomica, cited in full passage)</cite>

The passage elaborates the Stoic-Platonic concept of cosmic *spiritus*: without the single animating intelligence, the stars would lose their courses, night and day would cease to alternate, and the cosmos would dissolve. Zorzi uses Manilius as a pagan witness to the Johannine and Hermetic claim.

---

## IV. Platonic Self-Motion and the World-Animal

All Platonists, Zorzi asserts, affirm this doctrine as their "peculiare dogma." He extracts from Plato the argument concerning two kinds of motion: self-motion (*motus proprius*), which is more divine, belongs to souls alone; and externally-induced motion. Since the warmth of the world moves itself spontaneously, it must be an *animus* — the world is therefore animate and intelligent.

This raises the obvious objection: if the world is an animal, why does it not age, fall ill, and die? Zorzi resolves this by citing Calcidius's commentary on the *Timaeus*:

> «Ciò che Dio stabilisce è del tutto immune da ogni eventuale mutamento attribuibile al tempo, a malattie, alla vecchiaia o alla morte»

<cite>p. 1311 (Calcidius, In Timaeum)</cite>

The argument proceeds: things created immediately by God (without secondary causes or material intermediaries) — the heavens, the cosmic mechanism, the human soul — are eternal and imperishable. The world, moreover, is constituted from all possible parts; nothing external can enter to disturb it; what flows within it circulates back, "riposate e rinvigorite," into the universal nature.

---

## V. Chapter 2: The Fountain of Dual Life (Natural and Spiritual)

The second chapter (p. 1312 ff.) takes up the question of the source of life. Zorzi identifies a single fountain (*fons duplicis vitae*) — the God of Jeremiah ("Me dereliquerunt fontem aquarum viventium") — from which flow both natural and spiritual life. The river of Genesis ("Et fluvius egrediebatur de loco voluptatis") is the divine Logos distributing life through the Archetype via diverse channels; the "supramundane waters above the heavens" cited by Moses and David are life at its most primary level.

The Trinitarian structure is made explicit: the waters of life reside first in the Father (the paternal source), then in the Son (who is himself "fons apud Patrem" — Ps. 36:10: "quoniam apud te est fons vitae"), and finally in the Holy Spirit (Ezekiel's "spiritus vitae erat in rotis"; Ps. 104:30: "emitte spiritum tuum et creabuntur"). All three fountains are one in the divine essence.

Zorzi then marshals Apuleius of Madaura (*De Platone*, drawing on the "officina platonica") as a Platonic witness to divine omnipresence and the graduated diffusion of divine power through creation:

> «Le cose che vengono per prime, traggono una potenza maggiore, perciò i corpi celesti, quanto più sono vicini a Dio, tanto più ne ricevono; mentre ne ricevono in misura minore quelle che vengono per seconde, e così via fino alle realtà terrene le quali, con gradi ridotti, ricevono l'indulgenza di Dio il cui beneficio giunge fino a noi.»

<cite>p. 1313 (Apuleius, cited in Italian translation)</cite>

This Neoplatonic emanative hierarchy — greatest proximity to God yields greatest participation in life — is then harmonised with Paul's image of the Church as a body (1 Cor. 12), whose soul and life diffuses into its members "a seconda delle loro esigenze e del loro grado di vicinanza, non in senso spaziale, ma in senso morale e simbolico."

---

## VI. Chapter 3: Christ as the Tree of Life

Chapter 3 ("Quid sit arbor vitae") opens with the observation that Christ is celebrated under many names — Lamb, Lion, Cornerstone, Word, Truth, Life — and that "albero della vita" is one such name. Zorzi develops the allegory systematically: the tree is rooted in the earth (Christ's roots are in the terra viventium); it was a shoot from dry ground (Isaiah 53:2, "ascendit ut virgultum de terra"); it divides first into few but great branches (the apostles) and then into innumerable smaller ones; it draws life upward from the root to flowers and fruit.

The Johannine and Eucharistic resonances are explicit: John 15:5 ("qui manet in me et ego in eo, hic fert fructum multum"), John 6:57 ("sicut misit me vivens Pater et ego vivo propter Patrem"), John 6:58 ("qui manducat me… ipse vivet propter me"), John 15:6 ("mittetur foras sicut palmes et arescet").

---

## VII. Chapter 4: Where the Tree Is Planted — Hebrew mi-qedem

Chapter 4 addresses the location of the Tree of Life. Zorzi makes a notable Kabbalistic philological move: the paradise was planted *mi-qedem* (Hebrew rendering given in the text with transliteration), which he glosses as "dall'inizio ovvero da tempo immemorabile" — the entire garden and all true delight is rooted in the eternal Father as "origine prima di ogni bene," or alternatively, in the divine essence that is the vera terra viventium. The Word as second person of the Trinity occupies the middle position (persona media in the Trinity), which is why the Tree of Life stood "in medio paradisi" — a structural-theological correspondence between cosmic and Trinitarian geometry.

Zorzi adds a biographical-Christological dimension: Christ was born in medio animalium, disputed in medio doctorum, died in medio latronum, and rose again in medio discipulorum — the "middle" as the structural position of the mediator.

---

## Connections to Zorzi's Larger Project

This chunk exemplifies the synthetic method of *De Harmonia Mundi*: Hermetic, Platonic (Plato, Calcidius, Apuleius), Neoplatonic, Orphic, Stoic (Manilius), and Scriptural-Kabbalistic sources are made to speak in unison on a single theme. The *concordia* of traditions is itself the argument. The move from cosmic World-Soul to the Tree of Life as the Trinitarian Logos-mediator enacts Zorzi's characteristic conflation of macrocosmic metaphysics with Christological typology, with Hebrew philology (mi-qedem) providing the structural bridge between Kabbalistic and Christian Neoplatonic frameworks.
